entry.title: iniBuilds Publishes Massive New Aircraft Development Update
entry.summary: Sim: FS24. In a post on their forums, iniBuilds have shared a big update on the development progress of each of their aircraft projects, including the A220, A380, and TriStar for Microsoft Flight Simulator. ‍ The iniBuilds team expands on areas covered in their initial “Welcome to the TriStar” video published last week. One of the big aspects of the TriStar is the three-person cockpit, and iniBuilds have fully simulated this experience through the addition of a Virtual Flight Engineer. Users will be able to activate different flight engineer flows depending on the state of flight, with the flight engineer making relevant callouts and switch sounds playing in the background. Additionally, the iniBuilds TriStar will have automated cabin crew reacting to phases of flight, in what they claim will replicate the “feeling of a fully-operating airliner”. Another major feature on the TriStar is the “Paper Flight Bag” (PFB), a period-correct take on modern Electronic Flight Bags. The PFB will feature everything that users have come to expect in iniBuilds’ previous EFBs - such as SimBrief integration, performance calculations, ground services, customisation options, and loadsheet - as well as controls for the Virtual Flight Engineer, all made to look like real sheets of paper. For the TriStar’s soundset, iniBuilds have partnered with Echo19, and were able to record a real L-1011 airframe to recreate the sounds of the cockpit, cabin, and Rolls-Royce RB211 engines. On the visual side, iniBuilds have spent quite a lot of time working on the distinctive appearance of the TriStar, with much emphasis put on the cabin details and lighting to replicate the ‘golden-era’ aesthetics of airliners at the time, including an optional lounge on the lower deck. Also included is a “POD” variant used to carry a spare fifth engine below the right wing. This will affect the performance of the aircraft with changes being made to procedures and limitations, as well as being reflected in the flight model. The iniBuilds TriStar is set to be released this month, but with no exact release day or pricing being announced yet. At launch, it will be available on the Microsoft Flight Simulator Marketplace, with an iniManager release coming later. FSWeekend 2026 attendees will be able to test it out at iniBuilds’ booth this weekend in Lelystad, Netherlands. ‍ Synaptic Simulation’s upcoming rendition of the Airbus A220 has been progressing well, and approaching release-candidate status. Since their last development showcase at FSExpo 2025, much work has been made by the Synaptic team to overhaul the aircraft’s systems. The A220 now includes a completely reworked fly-by-wire system, a custom engine model, lateral and vertical flight guidance, autopilot, autoland, and fully featured electrical, bleed air, hydraulics, and radio systems. Most notably, the entire flight management system has been rewritten from scratch, which has allowed them to model more advanced interactions between systems, such as automatic navigation and engine model predictions, as well as improved performance. This includes their custom Crew Alerting System - with over 3,000 inputs and accurate timing delay - all using the same logic used in the real aircraft. The Synaptic and iniBuilds teams say that they are about to enter the final stages of testing at the end of the month. ‍ Finally, the iniBuilds team discussed their next big project: the highly anticipated Airbus A380 for Microsoft Flight Simulator. The A380 has been in development for months, and iniBuilds says that they are using the lessons learned from their previous large airliner releases, such as the A340/A350 and Tristar, to guide their development processes. Work on systems and sounds are underway, laying the foundation for the aircraft’s core functionality and overall flight experience. The team have focused on getting high-quality reference materials for every aspect of the aircraft, from systems to visuals and sounds. They have made multiple trips to capture reference photography, measurements, and 3D scans from real aircraft and full-motion simulators to help faithfully recreate the look of the A380. ‍ To celebrate the upcoming TriStar release and FSWeekend, they are running a sale on a range of their long-haul aircraft for Microsoft Flight Simulator iniBuilds have promised they will share more as development of these exciting new aircraft continues, and Threshold will keep you updated every step of the way, so stay tuned! ‍ If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon!

entry.title: RAVO Flight Systems Reveals First Product, 737NG Overhead Panel FWD
entry.summary: Sim: OTHER. A new hardware name has emerged ahead of FSWeekend 2026, with RAVO Flight Systems formally revealing its first product, the 737NG Overhead Panel FWD. This is a full-size forward overhead replica, aimed at home cockpit builders, and training setups. The unit is being sold through Aerosoft, with pre-orders now open, and first deliveries expected from June 2026. According to the product listing, the panel supports Microsoft Flight Simulator 2020 and 2024, X-Plane 11 and 12, and Prepar3D v4 and v5, with plug-and-play connection over USB or Ethernet through RAVO’s bridge software. Launch compatibility includes PMDG’s 737 lines, ProSim738, and the Zibo Mod, while support for the IXEG 737-300, and iFly 737 MAX, is planned after release. They also state that the hardware has been developed around EASA FNPT II and FAA FTD Level 5 design standards, and uses a modular layout that allows individual sections to be expanded, replaced, or removed. The package includes the overhead itself, mounting rails, a 3-metre USB-C to USB-A cable, and power adapters for EU, UK, and USA sockets. Optional electromagnetic switch upgrades, including engine start switches, yaw damper, wing anti-ice, and voice recorder, are planned for summer 2026. If you want to see the panel in person, RAVO says it will be demonstrating the unit at Aerosoft’s booth 33 during FSWeekend in Lelystad, March 21-22. You can pre-order a unit for yourself on the Aerosoft Store for aproximately $3824 USD, excluding taxes. ‍ If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon!

entry.title: FSWeekend 2026: Microsoft Announces City Update 14, AAU4, and Fokker F27 Friendship
entry.summary: Sim: FS24. Microsoft took to the stage at Flight Sim Weekend 2026 in Lelystad this morning with quite an informative keynote, even though Head of Microsoft Flight Simulator Jorg Neumann could not attend in person at the last minute. First up was City Update 14: The Netherlands and Belgium, which is available now, free of charge, for owners of both Microsoft Flight Simulator 2020 and Microsoft Flight Simulator 2024. The update adds 10 high-fidelity areas of interest built with TIN surface texturing, with eight locations in the Netherlands, including Amsterdam, Rotterdam, Eindhoven, and Utrecht, and two in Belgium, Antwerp and Bruges. Microsoft also confirmed that Aircraft and Avionics Update 4, or AAU4, will arrive with Sim Update 5 in “a few weeks”. This next AAU updates five aircraft to Microsoft Flight Simulator 2024-native standards: the ATR 42-600 and 72-600, the Antonov An-2, the Fokker F.VII, the Latecoere 631, and the Savoia-Marchetti S.55. Microsoft added that more Microsoft Flight Simulator 2020 aircraft are expected to be brought forward in future AAU packages as well. Of those, the ATR looks to be one of the biggest parts of the update, with Microsoft highlighting a native Microsoft Flight Simulator 2024 version, Career Mode support, navigation and autopilot improvements, TCAS refinements, and a new variant. AAU4 also adds the Avidyne IFD540 and IFD550 as new avionics options for developers, with Working Title presenting them as drop-in alternatives to the older GNS 500 family, and new Cessna 172 Classic variants fitted with those units. Finally, Microsoft closed with Local Legend 23, the Fokker F27 Friendship, developed by iniBuilds. The Dutch-built regional turboprop is available now in the in-sim marketplace for both Microsoft Flight Simulator 2020 and Microsoft Flight Simulator 2024 at $19.99 USD. If you are on the show floor in Lelystad this weekend, Microsoft says AAU4 content is also playable at booth 1. See the floorplan for details. For more information, see the Microsoft Flight Simulator home page. ‍ The Threshold team will continue to cover the news from FSWeekend 2026, live as they break from the show floor. ‍ ‍If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon!

entry.title: FSWeekend 2026: WinCtrl Announces New Products
entry.summary: Sim: OTHER. WinCtrl has just wrapped up a seismic presentation at FSWeekend 2026 in Lelystad, showcasing a flurry of new products. ‍ WinCtrl impressed the audience with the announcement of the OVHD32 32 SOP, a fully functional overhead panel for the A320. Although specific details about the panel were limited, WinCtrl stated that it will include true-to-scale switches, knobs, and indicators, along with highly realistic control feedback. The OVHD32 SOP is priced at $349.95. WinCtrl has announced the release of new joystick bases. The belt-driven EB Edition will be available in two versions: 23 Nm and 64 Nm. Additionally, the Direct Drive ED version will deliver 16 Nm of torque. The pricing for the belt-driven versions is set at $414.95 for the 23 Nm version and $699.95 for the 64 Nm version. The Direct Drive ED edition will also be available for $699.95. All of the bases are set to have an impressively small footprint. WinCtrl also showcased their Cyber Taurus Rudder Pedals, which now deliver 195 Nm of torque, up from the previous 140 Nm. An even more powerful version was also introduced, offering 590 Nm of torque. The 195 Nm model, named the EB195, is priced at $499.95, while the more powerful EB590, with 590 Nm of torque, will retail at $799.95. WinCtrl continued their presentation by showcasing the Cyber Taurus Yoke, a 1:1 scale replica of a 737 control wheel. According to WinCtrl, the yoke is designed using real data from full flight simulators used in pilot training. They also emphasised that the yoke will provide an “authentic control feel” with 35 cm of travel. A new yoke in the $400-500 segment was also teased, with more details coming soon. WinCtrl has been selling Airbus sidesticks for a long time, but until now, they have never offered a metal version. The new 3XO Metal Fly-By-Wire Joystick Grip is completely made of metal, as the name suggests, and will feature dynamic vibrations. This grip will be compatible with the Orion2 and the new Cyber Taurus Force Feedback bases. It will retail for $69.95 later this year. WinCtrl has not overlooked military aviation enthusiasts. The new Ursa Minor Fighter HOTAS will include a metal core mechanism, realistic trim, and RGB lighting. The base version will retail for $154.95, while the price for the full version has yet to be announced. WinCtrl has announced the next-generation ViperAce throttle grip, designed to provide an authentic aircraft control feel, enhanced dial realism, and a vibration module. The ViperAce Throttle Grip 2 will be available starting in April, priced at $244.06. WinCtrl were keen to show of their next generation MIP UFC Panel 2. The main updates include improved materials, enhanced durability, and a better feel. For example, silicone buttons have been replaced with hard keys. WinCtrl claims that this product will have a longer lifespan, even stating that it is "wear-resistant and aging-proof." It will be available staring in May from $195.6. SimApp Pro is getting a significant update. SimApp Pro 2 will have a completely new user interface. WinCtrl states that the software has been entirely rebuilt to provide a new optimized experience. Aircraft developers have also been brought in as partners. Keep an eye on Threshold for the latest updates from FSWeekend and flight simulation! ‍ If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon! ‍

entry.title: FSWeekend 2026: BlueBird Simulations Reveals 757 Trailer
entry.summary: Sim: FS24. BlueBird Simulations used FSWeekend 2026 in Lelystad to share a trailer for its long-awaited Boeing 757-200, while also reaffirming that this aircraft remains on track for release in Q3 2026. According to the developer, the model shown in the video was already around two weeks old by the time the trailer was presented, suggesting the project has continued to move forward even beyond what we saw on stage. Alongside the new video, BlueBird highlighted several features now in place, including dynamic virtual cockpit night lighting, more than 200 working circuit breakers, custom-coded autothrottle and flight controls, and a functional P61 panel. The developer also said Stage 2 of beta testing is set to begin in the first week of April, pushing toward release later this year. ‍ ‍If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon!

entry.title: FSWeekend 2026: Moza Announces New Flight Sim Hardware
entry.summary: Sim: OTHER. MOZA returns to FSWeekend 2026 with a slew of new hardware for their flight simulation ecosystem. At this year’s event, MOZA showcased several new cockpit products spanning multiple different niches, including the MA3F Electronic Flight Control Module, MA3F Flight Computer Display, MGX1000 Instrument Panel, and FMP18 Panel System. The MA3F Electronic Flight Control Module (EFCM) focuses on advanced autopilot controls. Inspired by the automation control interfaces used in modern aircraft flight decks, the MA3F allows pilots to manage key flight parameters such as speed, altitude, and navigation modes through tactile rotary encoders and digital display readouts. The MA3F also introduces an extended functionality mode, allowing additional aircraft systems to be mapped directly to the panel. ‍ Working alongside the EFCM, the MA3F Flight Computer Display (FCD) provides a dedicated hardware interface for flight management and navigation input. The device features a high-resolution display, illuminated keypad, and metal front panel designed to replicate real cockpit avionics. Users can use the interface for route entry, navigation management, aircraft configuration, and operational planning. Built-in checklist pages and note panels add additional functionality for complex flight operations. ‍ The MGX1000 Instrument Panel brings a modern glass cockpit avionics experience to flight simulation. The MGX1000 features a 10.4-inch IPS display, tactile rotary encoders, and a layout designed to mirror modern digital instrument panels. Multiple MGX1000 panels can also be combined to recreate complete primary and multifunction instrument displays. ‍ The FMP18 Panel System expands the MOZA ecosystem into combat flight simulation with a modular cockpit control interface inspired by modern combat aircraft. The system combines multi-function display frames, central control panels, and illuminated switches into a configurable cockpit interface. Backlit buttons, rotary encoders, and toggle switches provide clear tactile confirmation, allowing pilots to operate aircraft systems during challenging combat scenarios. ‍ All the new hardware is controlled by MOZA’s in-house Cockpit software, just like their existing products. The software enables pilots to customize input mappings, adjust control parameters, manage lighting effects, and synchronize telemetry across connected devices. ‍ No details on price point or an estimated release date have been announced yet, but Threshold will keep you updated when they do, so stay tuned! If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon!

entry.title: FSWeekend 2026: Aerosoft’s Stuttgart to Release on the 23rd
entry.summary: Sim: FS24. Aerosoft has recently announced the release date (23/03) for their rendition of Stuttgart Airport (EDDS) for Microsoft Flight Simulator 2024, serving the city of Stuttgart, Germany, with an average of 7 million passengers per year. Built in 1939 to replace Böblingen Airport, it was taken over by the American forces right after the war. It was partially returned in 1948: the runway and facilities were shared with the US Army during the Cold War. The original terminal was replaced in 2004 with four terminals capable of handling up to 12 million passengers annually. ​ Eurowings and Turkish Airlines use Terminal 1; Terminal 2 is used by Lufthansa and Star Alliance partners; Terminal 3 is used by TUIfly and KLM, and Terminal 4 is a separate building for other airlines, set to be rebuilt and expanded in the future. ​ It’s a hub for Condor, Eurowings, and TUI fly Deutschland, sporting a substantial list of destinations. ​ The scenery features an accurate rendition of the airport, with custom ground textures, custom jetways, a high-fidelity terminal interior, and much more. Furthermore, there’s an option to also acquire a GA bundle along with the airport, bringing 6 airfields from the vicinity (EDTQ, ED41, EDZT, EDSV, EDSG, and EDST). ​ The airport will retail for $22.49, and the bundle with the GA sceneries will cost $29.99. If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon! ‍

entry.title: FSWeekend 2026: Aerosoft/Toliss A346 Pro “to be Updated soon”, says Aerosoft
entry.summary: Sim: FS24. On the first day of FSWeekend, Aerosoft talked about their newly released A340-600 Pro, built in partnership with Toliss, and their plans for the future regarding updates and improvements. As promised during one of their official streams, the preighter (P2F) model is going to be “one of the next updates”, completely free of charge for current and future owners. The wing model - a point of criticism that was brought up multiple times during the first preview streams - will be rebuilt to look closer to the real counterpart, and the fuselage model will also undergo improvements. Furthermore, the Electronic Flight Bag will see improvements, with the charts feature set to undergo major tweaks, including the ability to pin charts. An issue with incorrect vertical speed and landing rate readings at touchdown was also acknowledged, and tweaks will be implemented in the future to ensure correct figures. They have also made sure to reinforce that they have been listening - and gathering - user feedback to address it when possible. ​ The first update is already undergoing testing and should be released “maybe within the next week,” and the current discount will end once that update is released. ​ Some improvements from the MSFS version will be carried over to X-Plane in future updates. ​ It’s available on Aerosoft’s website for roughly $80.75, requiring at least 18 GB of free hard disk space to install. If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon! ‍

entry.title: FSWeekend 2026: Meridian GMT Announces New Product Lineup
entry.summary: Sim: OTHER. Meridian GMT has taken the opportunity during FSWeekend 2026’s first day to announce a new product lineup, including a hybrid throttle quadrant and flight control unit, a new yoke, and two-purpose-built flight sim controllers. The Latitude Plus ($350) is their new multi-purpose throttle quadrant, offering four variants for distinct purposes: to begin with, there’s an Airbus-like (Toulouse) design and a Boeing-like (Everett) design, each with multi- and single-engine options. Furthermore, they can be fitted with a servo motor to motorize the trim wheel and throttles, and spoilers for an additional $49.99. The Horizon Pro ($599.99) is their new dual-axis force feedback yoke system, set to launch in early 2027 with a built-in FCU and other useful controls, including lights, basic MFD interaction buttons, and more. ​ The X-Ray Plus ($149.99) is their new purpose-built Xbox controller, engineered for flight simulation, bringing two distinct configurations: airplanes and helicopters, and their equivalent set of required buttons. Landing gear control, flaps, trim, all from one single controller, sized just the same as your average Xbox controller. The design is still subject to change. Please refer to Meridian’s website for further information. If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon! ‍

entry.title: FSWeekend 2026: Navigraph Showcases Three New Features
entry.summary: Sim: OTHER. Navigraph used its FSWeekend 2026 stage slot to preview three new additions coming soon to Navigraph Charts: Approach Feathers, Extended RWY Centerlines, and a new Measurement Tool. Navigraph has also been running live demos at the show throughout the weekend. Of the three, Approach Feathers is likely the headline feature. It is designed to surface key approach information directly on the chart, including items such as approach type, course, frequency, and identifier, helping reduce workload during the arrival phase. The ability to project the Extended RWY Centerline, meanwhile, is also a handy feature arriving in Navigraph Charts in the future. The third addition, the Measurement Tool, will let users measure distance either directly from point to point, or across multiple selected points, which should make it useful for everything from quick situational checks to more manual route planning. All three features are expected in the next Navigraph Charts release, which the presentation described as coming soon. ‍ Follow Threshold on Sunday, March 22, for more exciting news from FlightSimWeekend 2026. ‍ ‍If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon!

entry.title: FSWeekend 2026: Day 1 and Day 2 Recap
entry.summary: Sim: OTHER. FlightSimWeekend 2026 is now well underway in Lelystad, and, after Friday’s Captain’s Dinner and Flight Sim Awards, Saturday brought the first full public day of the show, complete with long queues, a packed theatre, and plenty to see on the floor. Friday, March 20, was centred around the Captain’s Dinner, followed by the Flight Sim Awards 2026. Hosted by KatiePilot and NickFlightX, with a pre-show by TwoToneMurphy, the evening mixed some very good food, music, community, and, of course, the awards. Among the notable winners were FlyByWire’s A380X, the Zibo 737 Mod, iniBuilds’ A350 Airliner, FlightFactor’s 777 Freighter, and Fenix Simulations. Saturday morning then kicked off the show proper at the Aviodrome, and it began in very, let’s say, Dutch fashion, with thick fog and low visibility outside the venue. Even so, the lines were long before opening. On stage, Microsoft opened the festivities. Although Jorg Neumann could not attend in person at the last minute, Microsoft still delivered a keynote confirming City Update 14: The Netherlands and Belgium, which is now available free of charge for MSFS 2020 and MSFS 2024. The team also confirmed that Aircraft and Avionics Update 4 will arrive with Sim Update 5 in a few weeks, bringing updates to the ATR, Antonov An-2, Fokker F.VII, Latecoere 631, and Savoia-Marchetti S.55. On top of that, Microsoft introduced new Avidyne avionics options, and surprise-released the Fokker F27 Friendship by iniBuilds as Local Legend 23. WinCTRL brought a large hardware showcase, including a new A320 overhead panel, new joystick bases, yoke, updated rudder pedals, and more. BlueBird Simulations shared a new trailer for its long-awaited Boeing 757-200, while reaffirming a Q3 2026 release window. Navigraph previewed three upcoming Charts features, Approach Feathers, Extended RWY Centerlines, and a Measurement Tool. Meridian GMT introduced some new hardware of its own, Aerosoft talked about Stuttgart for MSFS 2024 and future A340-600 Pro updates, and MOZA rounded things off with several new cockpit products. Away from the theatre, we spent the day moving around the venue, speaking to exhibitors, filming products, and taking in the views. We checked out everything from Microsoft and MOZA to OSM Aviation Academy, and one of the more eye-catching setups on the floor was perhaps HomeSim’s ProSim A320 family cockpit. We also recorded interviews with Vector, ahead of their Sunday session on the Project Griffin 787, and with WinCTRL, which has arrived at this year’s show with a very large product line-up. Elsewhere, Honeycomb even hosted a Kahoot session for attendees, with prizes up for grabs, adding a bit of extra fun between the larger stage presentations. FSWeekend 2026 has had a strong start. With Sunday still to come, there is still lots more on the way. Stay tuned on Threshold for all the latest from the event. ‍ If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon!

entry.title: Aerosoft Releases Stuttgart Airport for MSFS 2024
entry.summary: Sim: FS24. Aerosoft has recently released their rendition of Stuttgart Airport (EDDS) for Microsoft Flight Simulator 2024, serving the city of Stuttgart, Germany, with an average of 7 million passengers per year. Built in 1939 to replace Böblingen Airport, it was taken over by the American forces right after the war. It was partially returned in 1948: the runway and facilities were shared with the US Army during the Cold War. The original terminal was replaced in 2004 with four terminals capable of handling up to 12 million passengers annually. Eurowings and Turkish Airlines use Terminal 1; Terminal 2 is used by Lufthansa and Star Alliance partners; Terminal 3 is used by TUIfly and KLM, and Terminal 4 is a separate building for other airlines, set to be rebuilt and expanded in the future. ​It’s a hub for Condor, Eurowings, and TUI fly Deutschland, sporting a substantial list of destinations. The scenery features an accurate rendition of the airport, with custom ground textures, custom jetways, a high-fidelity terminal interior, and much more. Furthermore, there’s an option to also acquire a GA bundle along with the airport, bringing 6 airfields from the vicinity (EDTQ, ED41, EDZT, EDSV, EDSG, and EDST). The airport is available on Aerosoft’s shop for $24.99, and the bundle with the GA sceneries retails for $29.99. If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon!

entry.title: FSWeekend 2026: Vector Shares New 787 Details, Confirms -8 and -10
entry.summary: Sim: FS24. Vector returned on March 23 to the stage at FSWeekend 2026, giving a further update on its upcoming Boeing 787, codenamed “Project Griffin”. The update gave a clearer picture of both technical direction and the envisioned roadmap. The newly announced studio, revealed only weeks ago, describes itself as a team of more than 20 developers, including real-world 787 pilots, engineers, and experienced developers. According to Vector, development has been ongoing for over a year. During the presentation, Ellie, Head of Vector Studios, showcased the external modelling of the 787-9, including close-up engine detail, as well as some additional visuals. The aircraft is also being designed with Microsoft Flight Simulator 2024’s walkaround mode in mind, allowing users to interact with panels and inspect the aircraft externally. The presentation had a clear emphasis on system depth. Vector described its approach to the project as “schematic-level simulation”, modelling all aircraft systems based on schematics and real-world documentation, which was illustrated by several technical charts shown on stage. The 787's EDW-technology (Electronically Dimmable Windows) is also being recreated, shown below. Vector extensively referenced access to training and maintenance manuals, alongside input from pilots and ground crew, to support this level of detail. They showed off the electrical and fuel system layouts, as well as pages from the synoptic displays in-sim. Vector also confirmed integration with SimBrief and Navigraph, with users able to choose between the in-sim EFB or external services for flight planning. In a short Q&A session, Vector outlined plans for a configurable failures system, including both manual and random failures, supported by extensive checklists and maintenance pages. Persistent aircraft state is also being explored, although no firm details were provided. Additionally, early work is underway on SATCOM functionality, though the team noted that this remains an area of ongoing development. Looking ahead, Vector plans to share regular “snapshot” updates, focusing on individual areas such as systems, visuals, and soundscape. When confronted about the release timeline, Vector reaffirmed a 2026 release window. While no specific timeframe was given, Vector stated confidence in that target, noting that significant planning had taken place prior to development. Vector also confirmed that the 787-8 and 787-10, are planned. ‍ Keep yourself updated by joining the Vector Studio Discord. See the full presentation here. ‍ If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon!

entry.title: FSWeekend 2026: Day 3 Recap
entry.summary: Sim: OTHER. After a busy event, the final day of FSWeekend 2026 gave a more relaxed pace in Lelystad, with fewer major announcements, but plenty still happening across the floor. The main highlight from the theatre was perhaps Vector’s presentation, where the team shared further details on its Boeing 787, codenamed Project Griffin. The presentation focused on systems modelling, development strategy, and some long-term plans, including confirmation that the 787-8 and 787-10 are also planned for development. Read more on the Vector 787 here. The theatre, of course, continued with presentations from Virtual-Fly, Tailstrike.io, ExcellAir, and FlyByWire Simulations. Away from the stage, the final day was largely about taking in the show itself. The team spent time across the exhibition floor, speaking with developers and hardware manufacturers, including Navigraph, Just Flight, Skalarki, Meridian, StratoWare and others. We will feature some of these soon. We also recorded an interview with MOZA, which will be published on the site in the future. Outside the main halls, visitors had the opportunity to view historic aircraft on display, such as PH-BUK, a KLM Boeing 747-200M, alongside other aircraft, including the Fokker 100, MiG-21 and a Saab 37 Viggen. With that, Flight Sim Weekend 2026 comes to a close. This year’s event once again delivered a fantastic show in Lelystad, and we hope you enjoyed our coverage. We will have additional video coverage from the event coming soon. ‍ If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon!

entry.title: Orbx Shares Atlanta International Airport Development Update for MSFS 2020/2024
entry.summary: Sim: FS24. Orbx has recently shared more details about their ongoing work on Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport (KATL) for Microsoft Flight Simulator 2020 and 2024, now nearing completion. After a couple of obstacles during the development process, such as the conversion to Microsoft Flight Simulator 2024 standards, mesh issues, and the airport’s sheer size, they are finally approaching its eventual release date, scheduled to happen sometime around mid-April. While the original plan was to release it before Easter, the developer, Matteo Veneziani, wanted to ensure everything was as polished as possible before it reached the digital storefronts, hence the new tentative release window. ‍ They are on the final “to do” list, only needing to place a few 3D details (GSE, vehicles, trees, and people), export and place custom 3D taxiway signs, add custom approach and taxiway lighting, and complete the animations. It is said to be Matteo’s most detailed work yet, setting a new quality standard. Furthermore, they have also confirmed the existence of an X-Plane 12 version that is on the way, and shared a batch of screenshots from the MSFS version: If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon! ‍

entry.title: Atelic Releases Dresden Airport for MSFS 2020/2024
entry.summary: Sim: FS24. Atelic has recently released their rendition of Dresden Airport (EDDC) for Microsoft Flight Simulator 2020 and 2024, serving Dresden, Germany, with an average of 838,387 passengers per year. Dresden Airport opened to commercial traffic in 1935, quickly deviating from its civil airport purposes to become a Luftwaffe base, then exclusively military throughout the war, and later a Soviet educational center until its civil reopening in 1957. Between 1955 and 1961, Dresden Airport was home to the VEB Flugzeugwerke Dresden, a publicly-owned aircraft factory that didn’t really succeed, but at least increased the airport’s regional importance and shaped its current design. The unsuccessful factory was repurposed as a maintenance center for Eastern Bloc fighter aircraft and helicopters. After the reunification of Germany, the airport underwent massive expansion, and flights to western European capitals were added to the roster, leading to a sevenfold increase in traffic during the first half of the 1990s and the construction of a second terminal (inaugurated in 1995). In 2001, the current passenger terminal was inaugurated, built on top of what had been an aircraft factory (more of an assembly hall). It’s served by Air Cairo, Corendon Airlines, Eurowings, Lufthansa, Pegasus Airlines, Sky Alps, SunExpress, and Swiss International Air Lines. The scenery features an accurate rendition of the airport, with custom ground textures, an up-to-date ground layout, custom animated ground vehicles, a highly accurate terrain, high-quality PBR textures, custom jetways, a custom GSX profile, and more. It’s available on Contrail for roughly $20.67, requiring at least 4.73 GB of free hard disk space to install. If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon! ‍
